entry.title: Langflow CVE-2026-5027 Exploitation, Miasma Leak, and PeopleSoft ShinyHunters Attacks
entry.content: This digest window was dominated by active exploitation, developer ecosystem compromise, and policy movement around faster remediation of high-risk vulnerabilities. Reporting highlighted attacks against Langflow and Oracle PeopleSoft, continued evolution of self-propagating supply-chain malware, and defensive changes planned for npm in response to recent package ecosystem abuse. Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-5027, a high-severity path traversal flaw in Langflow that allows arbitrary file writes on exposed servers. The issue affects the POST /api/v2/files endpoint, where the filename parameter in multipart form data is not properly sanitized. Tenable disclosed the vulnerability on March 27, 2026, after reporting it to Langflow more than two months earlier without receiving a response; Snyk reported fixes in langflow-base 0.8.3 and Langflow 1.9.0, with 1.10.0 listed as the latest release. Separately, ShinyHunters claimed responsibility for ongoing data theft attacks against Oracle PeopleSoft cloud and on-premises customer instances. The group claimed to have stolen data from 300 PeopleSoft instances across more than 100 organizations, with education reportedly making up most of the affected sector. The actor described its exploitation path as a “gadget chain” involving old and zero-day vulnerabilities, with impact dependent on instance configuration. Researcher Michael R identified exposed online directories containing apparent attack tooling related to the PeopleSoft activity, including MeshCentral agents, a defacement and credential spray script, and staging materials. The two cases underscore continued attacker focus on externally reachable enterprise platforms where file write, deserialization, or configuration-dependent exploit chains can quickly become data theft or persistence opportunities. The Miasma credential-stealing framework was briefly exposed through GitHub repositories named “Miasma-Open-Source-Release” across numerous compromised developer accounts. SafeDep assessed that Miasma appears to be an evolution of the earlier Shai-Hulud worm, sharing many features, techniques, and code. The framework steals build environment and cloud credentials, compromises legitimate repositories and packages, and publishes trojanized versions to reach downstream developers. A notable feature of Miasma is its use of GitHub itself for command-and-control-like functions, reducing dependence on separate infrastructure. The leaked code also revealed a dead-man switch capable of deleting files in a user’s home and Documents folders if a stolen GitHub token used for exfiltration is revoked. This behavior combines credential theft, repository abuse, and destructive pressure against victims attempting to cut off attacker access. GitHub announced npm v12 security changes intended to disrupt similar supply-chain tactics. In npm v12, npm install will not run dependency preinstall, install, or postinstall scripts unless explicitly approved, and it will no longer fetch dependencies from Git repositories or remote URLs such as HTTPS tarballs unless permitted. GitHub said the defaults could have disrupted techniques seen in malicious install scripts, data-stealing npm packages, and Git dependency abuse documented in Shai-Hulud attacks. JFrog’s discussion of IDC’s 2026 Software Engineering Challenges Report framed Shadow AI as a growing unmanaged enterprise risk, replacing Shadow IT as developers use personal accounts, unvetted plugins, and AI-generated code in production workflows. The report, based on a survey of 1,000 enterprise and mid-sized organizations, described a widening gap between AI adoption goals and governance readiness. The article argued that AI agents cannot be assumed to self-police compliance because their behavior is non-deterministic and may bypass policies that exist only as documentation or informal agreements. Aikido described a parallel shift in where code is written, citing PostHog’s observation that roughly as many pull requests are being merged through Slack as through the code editor. The article said developers increasingly write code through AI agents, chat interfaces, and MCP servers that may be outside security team visibility. It connected this dispersion to recent supply-chain incidents such as TeamPCP activity, the Axios maintainer account hijacking, and the Glassworm attack, which emphasized compromise of developer devices and accounts rather than application vulnerabilities. Developer endpoints were characterized as high-value control points because they often hold cloud credentials, SSH keys, npm publish tokens, Kubernetes configs, source code access, and production access. The reporting also noted limitations in traditional MDM and EDR visibility for malicious packages, IDE extensions, browser plugins, and postinstall hooks before credential theft occurs. CISA issued a new binding operational directive requiring U.S. federal civilian agencies to patch certain high-risk vulnerabilities within 72 hours. The accelerated timeline applies when vulnerabilities meet three of four criteria: public internet exposure, presence in the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, automated exploitability, and the degree of adversary control over the vulnerable system. Agencies have 180 days to adopt the new time frame. The directive also requires agencies to assess when and how a vulnerable system may have been compromised before patching, reflecting CISA’s position that applying a patch does not generally evict an existing threat actor. CISA also urged state, tribal, and local governments, along with critical infrastructure owners and operators, to adopt similar practices.
entry.summary: This digest window was dominated by active exploitation, developer ecosystem compromise, and policy movement around faster remediation of high-risk vulnerabilities. Reporting highlighted attacks against Langflow and Oracle PeopleSoft, continued evolution of self-propagating supply-chain malware,...

entry.title: JDY Botnet, Langflow CVE-2026-5027 Exploitation, and Critical Ivanti/Fortinet/SAP Patches
entry.content: This coverage window was dominated by AI-adjacent security risk, active exploitation of exposed software, and renewed attention on edge and third-party access. Reporting also highlighted a China-linked reconnaissance botnet expanding across SOHO and IoT infrastructure, alongside a disruptive cyberattack affecting Australian sugar production. Multiple articles focused on how AI agents challenge existing zero trust and security inspection models. Varonis and Netskope both described AI agents as non-human actors that authenticate, call APIs, access databases, invoke tools, and exchange data at machine speed, creating gaps in policies designed around human access and authentication-time controls. Reported threat scenarios included prompt injection, tool poisoning, identity and privilege abuse, memory poisoning, supply chain compromise, and uninspected Model Context Protocol or app-to-LLM traffic. Software supply chain exposure in AI-assisted development also featured prominently. Socket announced an integration with Replit intended to evaluate open source packages as they enter AI-powered build workflows, citing threats such as typosquatting, impersonated packages, malicious transitive dependencies, credential stealers, and install scripts that retrieve second-stage payloads. The reporting reflects a broader shift in which AI agents can recommend, install, and wire dependencies directly into applications. SpecterOps published research on SQL Server 2025 AI-related features, demonstrating offensive use cases involving spinvokeexternalrestendpoint, CREATE EXTERNAL MODEL, and AIGENERATEEMBEDDINGS. The proof-of-concept activity included HTTPS exfiltration of table contents and files, trigger-based persistence for newly inserted data, NTLM coercion over SMB via UNC paths for ONNX models, and command-and-control patterns disguised as embedding-style traffic. Langflow CVE-2026-5027 stood out because it is reported as unpatched and under active exploitation. Tenable described the flaw as a path traversal issue in the POST /api/v2/files endpoint caused by insufficient sanitization of multipart filename values, while VulnCheck reported that default unauthenticated auto-login can allow attackers to obtain a session token and reach remote code execution. Censys data cited in the reporting identified roughly 7,000 internet-exposed Langflow instances, with most in North America. Patch reporting covered several critical enterprise products. Fortinet fixed CVE-2026-25089, a CVSS 9.1 command injection flaw in FortiSandbox products; Ivanti fixed CVE-2026-10520, a CVSS 10.0 unauthenticated root-level command injection issue in Ivanti Sentry, and CVE-2026-10523, a CVSS 9.9 authentication bypass enabling creation of arbitrary administrative accounts. SAP also released fixes for critical vulnerabilities affecting NetWeaver AS ABAP and ABAP Platform, SAP Commerce Cloud, SAP Data Hub, and NetWeaver Application Server Java, with no exploitation reported for those listed issues. CISA added Cisco, Chrome, and Arista vulnerabilities to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. The additions included CVE-2026-20245 in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, CVE-2026-11645 in Google Chrome V8, and CVE-2026-7473 in Arista EOS. Arista stated it does not plan to patch CVE-2026-7473 due to potential configuration breakage and instead documented ACL-based mitigations. Black Lotus Labs reporting, covered by both The Hacker News and BleepingComputer, described the China-linked JDY botnet as having expanded to more than 1,500 compromised SOHO and IoT devices. The botnet is used as a centrally controlled reconnaissance network for service discovery, banner grabbing, TLS certificate collection, protocol fingerprinting, and flaw-focused scanning after public vulnerability disclosures. The botnet has expanded beyond earlier Cisco RV320 and RV325 router activity to include devices from Araknis, Mimosa Networks, Ubiquiti, DrayTek, Hikvision, Linksys, and Cisco. Researchers said JDY places strong emphasis on U.S. targets, including U.S. military and associated networks. Command infrastructure reportedly uses Tor hidden services, and some activity involves the open-source Platypus reverse-shell and host-management framework. A cyberattack disrupted Mackay Sugar operations in Queensland, forcing shutdowns at the Farleigh and Racecourse mills and prompting growers to stop harvesting sugarcane until further communication. The company said it engaged cybersecurity experts and local authorities, implemented temporary measures to keep essential operations running, and was working to restore affected systems. Mackay Sugar did not disclose whether ransomware, data theft, or another form of malicious activity was involved. Identity and access management remained a parallel theme, particularly around third parties and account verification. Health-ISAC released a white paper on third-party IAM risks in healthcare, emphasizing vendor, partner, remote access, connected device, and facility management complexity. Separate reporting on identity verification cited a 160% surge in credential theft in 2025 and described phishing-resistant MFA, service desk verification, device trust signals, and passkeys as major areas of focus in modern identity programs.
entry.summary: This coverage window was dominated by AI-adjacent security risk, active exploitation of exposed software, and renewed attention on edge and third-party access. Reporting also highlighted a China-linked reconnaissance botnet expanding across SOHO and IoT infrastructure, alongside a disruptive...

entry.title: CVE-2026-42897, CVE-2026-45657, and AI-Enabled Phishing Drive June Threat Coverage
entry.content: This digest window was dominated by Microsoft’s unusually large June security release, including an actively exploited Exchange Server zero-day and a critical Windows kernel issue described as wormable. Reporting also highlighted continued evolution in phishing and initial access tradecraft, with AI-generated sites, adversary-in-the-middle kits, event-themed fraud, and low-content email payloads complicating detection. Microsoft patched CVE-2026-42897, an actively exploited high-severity spoofing vulnerability in Exchange Server affecting Exchange Server 2016, Exchange Server 2019, and Exchange Server Subscription Edition. The flaw can be exploited remotely without privileges by sending a specially crafted email that, under certain Outlook Web Access interaction conditions, executes arbitrary JavaScript in the user’s browser context. CISA added the vulnerability to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on May 15 and set a May 29 remediation deadline for U.S. federal agencies. Microsoft’s June Patch Tuesday fixed more than 200 security flaws, described by multiple vulnerability trackers as the largest monthly release in the program’s history. Among the most severe was CVE-2026-45657, a critical Windows kernel vulnerability rated 9.8 that could allow remote full system compromise without user interaction and was characterized by ZDI as wormable. The release also included CVE-2026-41091, an actively exploited Microsoft Defender elevation-of-privilege flaw that could allow an attacker with an existing foothold to gain full control of a system. The same Microsoft coverage included three disclosed zero-days, including CVE-2026-50507, a BitLocker bypass tied to the pseudonymous researcher Nightmare Eclipse. Separately, Microsoft reported that a small percentage of devices upgraded to Windows 11 versions 24H2 or 25H2 may fail to install the latest cumulative update, with errors including 0x80073712 and 0x800f0993 and logs referencing component store corruption or missing hydration candidates. Zscaler ThreatLabz reported that phishing activity declined by roughly 20% year over year in both 2024 and 2025, while attackers shifted toward more targeted and personalized lures resembling routine business workflows. The report identified 413,524 AI-generated site instances, with 37,447, or 9.06%, flagged as malicious, and attributed builders including Manus AI, Blackbox AI, and Anything AI. ThreatLabz also found that 95.2% of phishing activity was delivered over encrypted channels. The same research described phishing kits using adversary-in-the-middle and browser-in-the-middle techniques to capture credentials and MFA codes during live login flows, enabling session-level compromise rather than simple password theft. ThreatLabz also recorded 89.9 million hostile interactions with external decoys over six months and observed more than 121,000 distinct AWS-hosted IPs probing customer environments, underscoring the scale of opportunistic reconnaissance against exposed infrastructure. Event-themed fraud is already appearing around the FIFA World Cup 2026. The FBI warned that threat actors are creating fraudulent FIFA-affiliated websites to collect personal data, conduct financial fraud, and sell fake goods or services, while Cyble confirmed that many FBI-flagged domains remained active at publication time. Reported examples included ww-fifa[.]com, a fake hospitality and ticket portal requesting personal and payment details, and fifaworldcup-careers[.]com, a fake recruitment domain associated with multiple IP addresses and wildcard SSL certificates. IRONSCALES analyzed an “ACH payment” email sent from carlaburbo@keystonenatural[.]com to an accounts receivable team, with ap@keystonenatural[.]com copied. The message passed SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and composite authentication at final delivery, while original authentication results showed dkim=none and dmarc=none, and ARC validation at i=2 returned cv=fail, indicating a broken forwarding chain of trust. The email’s payload consisted entirely of a single PNG attachment named image002.png, with no message body, no links, and no OCR-extracted text. Themis flagged the message at 54% confidence as credential theft, and one mailbox was quarantined, illustrating how image-only payloads and authentication-chain inconsistencies can coexist with apparently successful final-delivery authentication outcomes. A related control-validation theme appeared in reporting on automated pentesting limitations. The article argued that clean automated pentest results can create false confidence because they show exploitable paths but not whether SIEM rules fired, EDR alerts were raised, or SOC analysts had sufficient signal to act. It contrasted automated pentesting with breach and attack simulation, framing the latter as focused on whether controls blocked, detected, logged, or missed known malicious behaviors.
entry.summary: This digest window was dominated by Microsoft’s unusually large June security release, including an actively exploited Exchange Server zero-day and a critical Windows kernel issue described as wormable. Reporting also highlighted continued evolution in phishing and initial access tradecraft, with...

entry.title: Microsoft YellowKey, phpBB RCE, ServiceNow Exploitation, and Ivanti Sentry Root RCE
entry.content: This coverage window is dominated by high-impact vulnerability disclosures affecting widely deployed enterprise and community platforms, including Windows, phpBB, ServiceNow, and Ivanti Sentry. Several items involve either confirmed exploitation, public proof-of-concept activity, or flaws that enable full system or administrative compromise. A parallel theme is the increasing role of advanced AI systems in vulnerability discovery and the controls vendors are placing around cyber-capable models. Microsoft’s June 2026 Patch Tuesday addressed three zero-day vulnerabilities known as GreenPlasma, MiniPlasma, and YellowKey. GreenPlasma and MiniPlasma are local privilege escalation flaws in the Collaborative Translation Framework and Cloud Files Mini Filter Driver, respectively, and can allow attackers to obtain SYSTEM privileges on fully patched Windows systems prior to remediation. YellowKey is a Windows Recovery Environment backdoor affecting unpatched Windows 11 and Windows Server 2022/2025 systems, enabling attackers with physical access to bypass BitLocker protections. The vulnerabilities were disclosed by a researcher using the handle Nightmare Eclipse, who framed the release as a protest against Microsoft Security Response Center disclosure practices. The same researcher has reportedly released proof-of-concept exploits for additional Windows zero-days, including BlueHammer, RedSun, UnDefend, and RoguePlanet. This creates a broader exposure pattern in which defenders face not only patched vulnerabilities, but also an expanding set of public exploit artifacts tied to unresolved or recently disclosed Windows flaws. Aikido reported a critical phpBB authentication bypass vulnerability that can lead to remote code execution and complete takeover of the underlying system. The flaw affects phpBB 3.3.16 and below, as well as 4.0.0-a2, and is exploitable in the default configuration with a single unauthenticated HTTP request to obtain a valid session as any user. phpBB released version 3.3.17 after the issue was reported through HackerOne on June 2, with a working patch published on June 6. The update also introduces a breaking change for OAuth authentication users because the redirect URI handler has moved to /user/oauth/authenticate/..., which may affect deployments relying on existing OAuth flows. Ivanti also disclosed two critical Sentry secure mobile gateway vulnerabilities. CVE-2026-10520 is a maximum-severity OS command injection flaw allowing remote code execution as root, while CVE-2026-10523 is an authentication bypass that can allow unauthenticated remote attackers to create rogue administrative accounts and gain full administrative access; Ivanti stated it had no evidence of exploitation at disclosure time. ServiceNow reported that unknown threat actors exploited a security issue to gain greater unauthorized access to susceptible customer instances than intended. The issue does not currently have a CVE identifier, and details first emerged publicly through Reddit before ServiceNow described anomalous activity and successful queries of instance tables affecting a subset of customers. ServiceNow applied a hosted-instance security update on June 5, changing an endpoint configuration to restrict access to authenticated users. The affected population includes customers on the Australia platform release and customers that made certain configuration changes to instances running releases prior to Australia. The incident is notable because it involves exploitation against a major enterprise workflow platform and successful access to customer instance data. ServiceNow said affected customers were notified after evidence of successful table queries was observed. Anthropic released Claude Fable 5 publicly while limiting its cyber-unrestricted counterpart, Claude Mythos 5, to vetted cyber defenders and critical infrastructure operators. The company said Mythos-class models can find and exploit software vulnerabilities at a level where unrestricted public access would provide attackers with significant uplift. Claude Fable 5 uses safety classifiers that route flagged cyber, biology, chemistry, and distillation requests to the weaker Claude Opus 4.8 rather than refusing them outright. Anthropic also said that during Project Glasswing, it and roughly 50 partners used Claude Mythos Preview to identify more than ten thousand high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities in systemically important software. Anthropic will require 30-day data retention for traffic on Fable 5, Mythos 5, and future models at this capability level, while stating that retained data will not be used for training or non-safety purposes. The release reflects a widening gap between public AI capabilities and restricted cyber-specialized systems intended for controlled defensive use.
entry.summary: This coverage window is dominated by high-impact vulnerability disclosures affecting widely deployed enterprise and community platforms, including Windows, phpBB, ServiceNow, and Ivanti Sentry. Several items involve either confirmed exploitation, public proof-of-concept activity, or flaws that...

entry.title: Proto6 protobuf.js RCE Flaws and AI Security Tooling Developments
entry.content: This digest window is dominated by two themes: newly disclosed Node.js ecosystem risk in protobuf.js, and continued maturation of AI tooling for security operations and research. The most immediately operationally relevant item is the Proto6 vulnerability set, which affects protobuf.js consumers across application, cloud library, messaging, and CI/CD contexts. Researchers at Cyera disclosed six vulnerabilities collectively named Proto6 in protobuf.js, a widely used JavaScript implementation for Protocol Buffers. The issues can expose affected Node.js applications to remote code execution and denial-of-service conditions, particularly where applications deserialize Protobuf data or generate code from schemas. The flaws center on protobuf.js treating schema and metadata as trusted by default. According to the reporting, this behavior can allow attacker-controlled schema-related input to influence application behavior and, in certain paths, reach code execution conditions. The most severe issue highlighted is CVE-2026-44291, which can allow arbitrary JavaScript execution when attacker-controlled input reaches a prototype pollution gadget before protobuf.js encodes or decodes a message. The affected exposure surface extends beyond direct protobuf.js use. The article identifies Google Cloud client libraries, messaging frameworks such as Baileys, Node.js services handling Protobuf data, and CI/CD pipelines that generate code from schemas as potentially impacted environments. Fixes are reported in protobufjs 7.5.6 and 8.0.2, along with protobufjs-cli 1.2.1 and 2.0.2. Several articles in this window focused on AI-enabled cybersecurity tooling and model governance rather than active intrusion activity. SpecterOps introduced GhostWorks, an AI-focused engineering and research initiative intended to test frontier AI capabilities against identity-security problems, document limitations, and transition successful prototypes beyond the lab. The initiative follows earlier SpecterOps AI work, including OpenAI adversarial red teaming and participation in OpenAI’s Trusted Access for Cyber program. SpecterOps also published a technical examination of GEPA, an optimization framework for refining prompts using LLM inference instead of reinforcement learning. The proof-of-concept optimized an AGENTS.md prompt for a web CTF agent with shell execution, file access, web request, and flag submission tooling. Its evaluation function rewarded solved challenges and weighted turn efficiency more heavily than runtime, using agent run artifacts such as reasoning output, tool use, return values, and errors to identify patterns. The GEPA demonstration produced mixed but informative results: one challenge score decreased slightly while two improved, with signs of overfitting attributed to the small training and validation set. The article positions GEPA as measurable prompt engineering for security agents, especially where practitioners use closed-source or remote-hosted models and have limited compute resources. Separately, Anthropic began rolling out Claude Fable 5, described as a safer version of a Mythos-class model with guardrails for sensitive domains including offensive cybersecurity, biology, and chemistry. The unrestricted Claude Mythos 5 model is reportedly limited to highly vetted trusted partners, including government cyberdefenders and selected life sciences researchers. BleepingComputer reported high usage consumption during testing, including approximately 1 million tokens in 8 minutes and rapid exhaustion of a $100 Max subscription daily allowance when using Workflow mode and high model thinking.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by two themes: newly disclosed Node.js ecosystem risk in protobuf.js, and continued maturation of AI tooling for security operations and research. The most immediately operationally relevant item is the Proto6 vulnerability set, which affects protobuf.js consumers...

entry.title: Langflow CVE-2026-5027, Microsoft Patch Tuesday, JDY Botnet, and Miasma Supply Chain Threats
entry.content:
This 24-hour digest is dominated by active exploitation, record vulnerability volume, and continued abuse of developer ecosystems. Reporting also highlights how AI-enabled development, agentic workflows, and software supply-chain complexity are expanding the attack surface across identity, data, endpoints, and build pipelines. Langflow CVE-2026-5027 emerged as a prominent active exploitation story, with attackers abusing a path traversal flaw in the POST /api/v2/files endpoint to write arbitrary files on exposed servers. Reporting from VulnCheck and others notes that unauthenticated auto-login behavior can turn the file write primitive into remote code execution, and internet exposure is significant, with Censys identifying roughly 7,000 public instances. Microsoft’s June 2026 Patch Tuesday was described by multiple trackers as the largest in the program’s history, covering more than 200 vulnerabilities. The release included critical RCE flaws in Windows Kernel, HTTP.sys, and DHCP Client, an actively exploited Microsoft Defender elevation-of-privilege bug, and several publicly disclosed zero-days associated with the Nightmare Eclipse persona, including BitLocker and Windows Recovery Environment bypass issues. CISA activity reinforced the operational significance of exploited vulnerabilities. The agency added Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, Google Chrome V8, Arista EOS, and Exchange Server flaws to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, while a new binding directive will require federal civilian agencies to patch certain high-risk vulnerabilities within 72 hours once adoption deadlines take effect. The developer ecosystem remained a major target. The Miasma credential-stealing framework was briefly exposed through GitHub repositories created on compromised developer accounts, revealing a GitHub-centric command-and-control model, a multi-stage obfuscated build pipeline, and a dead-man switch tied to revoked stolen tokens. A separate Rust ecosystem incident involved the compromised onering crate version 1.4.1, which added a Cargo build script that collected the consuming project’s latest commit metadata and code diff, then disguised exfiltration as Sentry telemetry. The maintainer’s GitHub repository also reportedly appeared compromised, indicating risk across both registry and source repository distribution paths. GitHub’s upcoming npm v12 security changes reflect broader pressure on package managers to reduce install-time execution risk. The new defaults will block dependency lifecycle scripts and remote dependency resolution unless explicitly approved, while Socket’s integration with Replit reflects a parallel move to inspect packages as AI-assisted development systems introduce dependencies during builds. Lumen’s Black Lotus Labs reported that the China-linked JDY botnet has expanded to more than 1,500 compromised SOHO and IoT devices. The botnet, previously associated with the KV-botnet ecosystem, is used for distributed scanning, banner collection, TLS certificate harvesting, and service fingerprinting, with observed emphasis on U.S. military and associated networks. ShinyHunters claimed responsibility for ongoing data theft attacks against Oracle PeopleSoft cloud and on-premises environments, alleging compromise of hundreds of instances across more than 100 organizations, with education heavily represented. Researchers also identified exposed tooling linked to the activity, including MeshCentral agents, credential spraying scripts, and staging materials. Operational disruption was reported in Australia, where Mackay Sugar shut down two major mills following a cyberattack that interrupted harvesting operations in Queensland. The company said cybersecurity experts and local authorities were involved in the investigation, but the incident type and any data impact were not disclosed. Several reports focused on AI agents as a control-plane and data-access risk. Varonis and Netskope both framed agentic systems as challenging traditional zero trust assumptions because authenticated agents can process malicious inputs after login, interact with databases and APIs directly, and exchange data over Model Context Protocol or app-to-LLM paths that may lack inspection. SpecterOps demonstrated offensive tradecraft involving SQL Server 2025 AI-related features, including use of spinvokeexternalrestendpoint for data exfiltration, triggers for automatic row-level leakage, UNC paths for NTLM coercion, and combinations of embeddings, external models, xpcmdshell, and SQL CLR assemblies for database-resident command-and-control. Industry commentary from JFrog and Aikido described governance lag around Shadow AI, AI-generated code, MCP servers, chat-based coding, and developer devices. The common theme is that code creation and dependency introduction are spreading beyond traditional IDE workflows, while developer endpoints continue to hold high-value credentials and production access. Zscaler ThreatLabz reported that phishing volume declined year over year while attacks became more targeted and personalized, with extensive use of encrypted channels, adversary-in-the-middle kits, browser-in-the-middle techniques, and AI-generated sites. Cyble also documented active FIFA World Cup 2026 scam infrastructure, including fake ticketing, hospitality, and employment domains. Identity-focused reporting highlighted persistent abuse of credentials, helpdesk processes, and device trust gaps. Health-ISAC published guidance on third-party IAM challenges in healthcare, while separate reporting emphasized phishing-resistant authentication, device posture signals, and biometric data handling as identity verification issues in environments facing AI-assisted social engineering and credential theft.

Microsoft Patches Record 206 Flaws, Including Three Zero-Days and Critical RCE Bugs — Must Read

Microsoft’s June 2026 Patch Tuesday fixes a record 206 vulnerabilities, including three publicly disclosed zero-days and several critical remote code execution flaws in Windows components such as Kernel, HTTP.sys, and DHCP Client. Organizations should prioritize patching internet- and network-facing Windows systems due to the risk of unauthenticated remote compromise and denial-of-service attacks.

  • Microsoft released fixes for a record 206 security vulnerabilities across its software portfolio, including 39 rated Critical, 167 rated Important, and three that were publicly disclosed at release.
  • The June 2026 patches include critical remote code execution vulnerabilities in Windows Kernel, Windows HTTP.sys, and Windows DHCP Client, with CVE-2026-45657, CVE-2026-47291, and CVE-2026-44815 each carrying a CVSS score of 9.8.
  • Microsoft addressed several BitLocker security feature bypass issues, including CVE-2026-45585 linked to the YellowKey proof-of-concept and CVE-2026-50507 assessed as a fix for the bitskrieg bypass.
  • CVE-2026-49160 is related to the HTTP2/Bomb denial-of-service technique, and Microsoft introduced a new MaxHeadersCount registry setting to limit HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 request headers.
  • The article says the growing volume of Microsoft patches has been attributed to AI-assisted vulnerability discovery, with researchers expecting vulnerability counts to continue rising.

Oracle PeopleSoft servers hacked in ShinyHunters data theft attacks — Must Read

ShinyHunters claims it is stealing data from Oracle PeopleSoft cloud and on-premises servers using a mix of old and possible zero-day flaws, with more than 100 organizations allegedly affected. PeopleSoft operators should check logs for the reported IP indicators and begin incident response if they see related activity.

  • The ShinyHunters extortion gang told BleepingComputer it is behind ongoing data theft attacks targeting Oracle PeopleSoft cloud and on-premises customer instances.
  • ShinyHunters claimed it stole data from 300 PeopleSoft instances across more than 100 organizations, with most impacted organizations in the education sector.
  • The threat actor said it is using a "gadget chain" of old and zero-day vulnerabilities, and that exploitation may depend on how a PeopleSoft instance is configured.
  • Cybersecurity researcher Michael R found exposed online directories containing attack-related tooling, including MeshCentral agents, a defacement and credential spray script, and staging materials.
  • Organizations running Oracle PeopleSoft are advised to analyze logs for connections from the listed IP addresses and begin incident response if the indicators are found.

Microsoft patches Exchange Server zero-day exploited in attacks — Must Read

Microsoft patched an actively exploited Exchange Server zero-day, CVE-2026-42897, that can let remote attackers run arbitrary JavaScript against Outlook Web Access users via crafted emails. Exchange admins should install the June 2026 security updates immediately and keep Microsoft’s temporary mitigation enabled for added protection.

  • Microsoft patched CVE-2026-42897, an actively exploited high-severity spoofing vulnerability in Exchange Server that enables cross-site scripting attacks against Outlook Web Access users.
  • CVE-2026-42897 affects Exchange Server 2016, Exchange Server 2019, and Exchange Server Subscription Edition, and can be exploited remotely by attackers with no privileges.
  • Microsoft said attackers could exploit the flaw by sending a specially crafted email that executes arbitrary JavaScript in the browser context if opened in Outlook Web Access and certain interaction conditions are met.
  • Microsoft advised administrators to install the June 2026 Exchange Server security updates as soon as possible and to keep the Exchange Emergency Mitigation Service mitigation in place for additional protection.
  • CISA added CVE-2026-42897 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on May 15 and ordered U.S. government agencies to patch affected servers by May 29.

Unpatched Langflow Flaw CVE-2026-5027 Exploited for Unauthenticated RCE — Must Read

An unpatched Langflow path traversal flaw, CVE-2026-5027, is being actively exploited and can allow unauthenticated remote code execution because default auto-login exposes the vulnerable file upload endpoint. About 7,000 internet-facing Langflow instances may be at risk.

  • A high-severity unpatched Langflow vulnerability tracked as CVE-2026-5027 is being actively exploited in the wild, according to VulnCheck.
  • CVE-2026-5027 is a path traversal flaw in the POST /api/v2/files endpoint that allows attackers to write files to arbitrary filesystem locations via unsanitized filename parameters.
  • VulnCheck said the flaw enables remote code execution because Langflow enables unauthenticated auto-login by default, allowing attackers to obtain a valid session token without credentials.
  • Exploitation observed so far appears to use the bug to write test files on victim systems.
  • Censys data shows about 7,000 Langflow instances are publicly exposed on the internet, with most located in North America.

CISA Adds Cisco, Chrome, and Arista Flaws to KEV Catalog Amid Active Exploitation — Must Read

CISA added actively exploited flaws in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, Google Chrome V8, and Arista EOS to its KEV catalog, requiring U.S. federal agencies to fix or mitigate them by June 23, 2026. Notably, Arista does not plan to patch its EOS tunnel decapsulation flaw and is recommending ACL-based mitigations instead.

  • CISA added CVE-2026-20245, CVE-2026-11645, and CVE-2026-7473 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after reports of active exploitation.
  • CVE-2026-20245 is an improper output encoding or escaping flaw in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager that could allow an authenticated local attacker to execute arbitrary commands as root using a crafted file.
  • CVE-2026-11645 is an out-of-bounds read and write vulnerability in Google Chrome V8 that could allow a remote attacker to execute arbitrary code inside a sandbox via a crafted HTML page.
  • CVE-2026-7473 affects Arista EOS and can cause affected switches configured as tunnel endpoints to incorrectly decapsulate and forward unexpected tunneled packets with a destination IP matching a configured decapsulation IP.
  • Arista said it does not plan to patch CVE-2026-7473 because of risks to existing deployments and instead recommended ACL-based mitigations.

China-linked JDY botnet expands targeting of U.S. military networks — Should Read

The China-linked JDY botnet has expanded to over 1,500 compromised SOHO and IoT devices and is being used to rapidly scan for newly disclosed vulnerabilities, with a strong focus on U.S. military and related networks. Defenders should patch edge devices, lock down remote management, and monitor for unusual outbound scanning.

  • Black Lotus Labs by Lumen reports that the China-linked JDY botnet has grown from roughly 650 active bots in January 2024 to over 1,500 compromised SOHO and IoT devices today.
  • JDY is a distributed scanning and fingerprinting network used to identify vulnerable infrastructure shortly after public vulnerability disclosures, rather than an exploitation framework or DDoS botnet.
  • Researchers observed that JDY maintains a strong focus on the United States, particularly targeting U.S. military and associated networks.
  • The botnet includes compromised devices from Cisco, Araknis, Mimosa Networks, Ubiquiti, DrayTek, Hikvision, and Linksys across several MIPS-based architectures.
  • JDY uses hidden Tor services for command-and-control, receives scanning assignments from a central Dispatch Service, and supports TCP, SSL/TLS, UDP, ICMP, banner collection, TLS certificate harvesting, and service fingerprinting.

Compromised Rust crate onering performs code exfiltration — Should Read

The Rust crate onering version 1.4.1 was compromised with a malicious build.rs script that exfiltrates the consuming project's latest Git commit metadata and source diff to a Sentry endpoint during builds. Users should avoid the affected crates.io and GitHub versions and check for outbound traffic to the listed Sentry ingest URL.

  • Aikido detected malicious behavior on June 10, 2026, in version 1.4.1 of the Rust crate onering, a synchronous queue and channels library with over 18,000 downloads on crates.io.
  • The compromised version added a build.rs file that runs during Cargo builds and locates the root of the project consuming the crate.
  • The malicious build script runs Git commands to collect the latest commit metadata and the full textual diff of the most recent commit from the consuming project.
  • The stolen commit metadata and code diff are disguised as a Sentry telemetry event and sent via curl to a Sentry ingest endpoint.
  • The article says the maintainer's GitHub repository also appears to be compromised, so pulling the crate from Git instead of crates.io does not make users safe.

The ‘Miasma’ worm source code briefly leaked on GitHub — Should Read

The Miasma credential-stealing worm source code was briefly and apparently deliberately published on GitHub, giving other attackers access to a self-propagating supply-chain attack framework. Researchers warn the leak could fuel more attacks against open-source ecosystems by helping threat actors steal developer credentials and compromise legitimate packages and repositories.

  • The Miasma credential-stealing attack framework was briefly open-sourced on GitHub through repositories named "Miasma-Open-Source-Release" on numerous compromised developer accounts.
  • Miasma infects developer machines, steals build environment and cloud credentials, and uses them to compromise legitimate repositories and packages with trojanized versions.
  • SafeDep found that Miasma requires no command-and-control infrastructure because it uses GitHub for that purpose.
  • The leaked source code revealed a dead-man switch that can delete files in a user's home and Documents folders if a stolen GitHub token used for exfiltration is revoked.
  • SafeDep reported that Miasma uses a five-stage build pipeline with encryption, obfuscation, source transformations, and randomized encoding to generate unique payloads for each build.

GitHub announces npm security changes to tackle supply-chain attacks — Should Read

GitHub’s upcoming npm v12 will block dependency install scripts, Git-based dependencies, and remote URL dependencies from running or resolving automatically unless explicitly approved. The change is meant to reduce npm supply-chain attacks that abuse npm install for automatic code execution.

  • GitHub announced that npm v12, expected next month, will introduce security-focused changes aimed at blocking supply-chain attacks that abuse behaviors triggered by the npm install command.
  • Starting in npm v12, npm install will not run preinstall, install, or postinstall scripts from dependencies unless they have been explicitly approved, including native module builds through node-gyp and prepare scripts from Git, local file, and linked dependencies.
  • npm install will no longer fetch dependencies from Git repositories or resolve dependencies installed from remote URLs, including HTTPS tarballs, unless they are explicitly permitted.
  • GitHub says the new defaults could have disrupted recent supply-chain attack techniques involving malicious install scripts, data-stealing npm packages, and Git dependency abuse documented in Shai-Hulud attacks.
  • GitHub recommends developers upgrade to npm 11.16.0 or newer to see warnings about actions that will break under npm v12 and identify workflows that will require explicit approval.

CISA to require federal agencies to patch some cyber vulnerabilities within 3 days — Should Read

CISA will require federal civilian agencies to patch the highest-risk, internet-facing and actively exploited vulnerabilities within 72 hours, with agencies given 180 days to implement the new process. The directive is meant to prioritize fixes for vulnerabilities that can be automated and rapidly exploited, especially as AI increases attacker capabilities.

  • CISA issued a new binding operational directive requiring federal civilian agencies to patch certain high-risk cyber vulnerabilities within 72 hours.
  • The 72-hour deadline applies to vulnerabilities that meet three of four criteria: public internet exposure, inclusion in the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, automated exploitability, and the level of adversary control over the vulnerable system.
  • Federal agencies have 180 days to adopt the new patching time frame under the directive.
  • The directive also requires agencies to check when and how a vulnerable system was compromised before applying a patch, because applying a patch generally does not evict a threat actor.
  • CISA is strongly urging state, tribal, and local governments, as well as critical infrastructure owners and operators, to adopt similar vulnerability management practices.

Oops, I Weaponized the Database: Abusing AI Features in SQL Server 2025 — Should Read

SQL Server 2025’s new AI features can be abused as built-in channels for data exfiltration, NTLM coercion, and command-and-control traffic from inside the database engine. Defenders should tightly restrict these features, monitor external model and REST endpoint use, and block unexpected SQL Server egress.

  • SQL Server 2025 introduces native AI-related features including sp_invoke_external_rest_endpoint, CREATE EXTERNAL MODEL, and AI_GENERATE_EMBEDDINGS.
  • The article demonstrates that sp_invoke_external_rest_endpoint can send database table contents or file contents to an external HTTPS endpoint with payloads up to 100 MB.
  • The article shows that a SQL trigger can use sp_invoke_external_rest_endpoint to exfiltrate newly inserted table rows whenever a change occurs.
  • The article describes how CREATE EXTERNAL MODEL with UNC paths for ONNX Runtime models can coerce NTLM authentication over SMB.
  • The article demonstrates combining AI_GENERATE_EMBEDDINGS, external models, xp_cmdshell, and SQL CLR assemblies to create command-and-control channels from within SQL Server.

Cyberattack shuts down major Australian sugar mills, disrupting harvest — Should Read

A cyberattack forced Mackay Sugar to shut down two major mills in Queensland, halting cane harvesting in one of Australia’s largest sugar-growing regions. The company is investigating with cybersecurity experts and authorities but has not disclosed the type of attack or whether data was compromised.

  • A cyberattack disrupted Mackay Sugar’s operations and forced the shutdown of its Farleigh and Racecourse mills in Queensland’s Mackay region.
  • Canegrowers Mackay said Mackay Sugar instructed growers to stop harvesting sugarcane immediately until further communication from the company.
  • Mackay Sugar said it engaged cybersecurity experts and local authorities to investigate the incident and safely restore affected systems.
  • The company said it implemented temporary measures to keep essential operations running and minimize disruption while restoration work continues.
  • Mackay Sugar did not disclose the nature of the cyberattack, whether any data was compromised, or whether ransomware or another form of malicious activity was involved.

FIFA World Cup 2026 Scams Are Already Active: Fake Domains, Phishing Sites, and How to Stay Safe — Should Read

Cybercriminals are already impersonating FIFA World Cup 2026 websites with fake ticket, hospitality, streaming, and job portals to steal personal and payment information. Fans and job seekers should only use www.fifa.com or official broadcasters, avoid sponsored or unsolicited links, and report suspected scams to IC3.

  • The FBI warned that threat actors are creating fraudulent FIFA-affiliated websites to steal personal information, conduct financial fraud, and sell fake products and services related to the FIFA World Cup 2026.
  • The FBI flagged numerous fraudulent FIFA-related domains using typosquatting and similar tactics, and Cyble researchers confirmed that many remained active at the time of publishing.
  • Cyble identified ww-fifa[.]com as a fake FIFA World Cup 2026 hospitality and ticket portal that used tournament branding and prompts for personal and financial information.
  • Cyble found that fifa[.]help was classified as malicious by 15 of 92 security vendors on VirusTotal, with detections including phishing and fraud categories.
  • Cyble identified fifaworldcup-careers[.]com as a fake FIFA employment portal targeting job seekers, with VirusTotal detections and infrastructure including multiple IP addresses and wildcard SSL certificates.

Microsoft: Some Windows PCs fail to install latest monthly updates — Should Read

Microsoft says some Windows devices upgraded to Windows 11 24H2 or 25H2 may fail to install the June 2026 cumulative updates with errors 0x80073712 or 0x800f0993. A restart should resolve the issue for many unmanaged and Home devices, while already affected systems may need to remove a specific update package or perform an in-place upgrade.

  • Microsoft warned that some Windows devices upgraded to Windows 11 version 24H2 or 25H2 may fail to install the latest monthly cumulative updates.
  • Affected systems can show Windows Update errors 0x80073712 or 0x800f0993 when attempting to install the June 2026 cumulative updates.
  • Microsoft said a fix will roll out to all unmanaged enterprise devices and Windows Home PCs after a system restart, with no new devices in those categories expected to be affected after May 19, 2026, 6:30 p.m. PT.
  • Microsoft released June 2026 Patch Tuesday updates intended to install automatically during upgrades to Windows 11 to prevent the issue from occurring on other affected devices.
  • For systems already upgraded to Windows 11 version 24H2 or 25H2, Microsoft advised removing the affected package with a DISM command, and performing a Windows 11 in-place upgrade if that mitigation does not work.

The Invoice Email With No Text to Scan: Image-Only Payload From a Compromised Account With a Broken ARC Chain — May Read

A compromised vendor email account sent an image-only “ACH payment” attachment to an accounts receivable team, passing SPF, DKIM, and DMARC at delivery while hiding weak original authentication and a broken ARC chain. The key risk is that fully authenticated mail from valid accounts can still carry opaque invoice-fraud payloads that evade text and link scanning.

  • An email with the subject “ACH payment” was sent from carlaburbo@keystonenatural[.]com to the accounts receivable team of a global flavoring company and CC’d ap@keystonenatural[.]com.
  • The message passed SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and composite authentication at final delivery, but Authentication-Results-Original showed dkim=none and dmarc=none, and ARC validation at i=2 returned cv=fail.
  • The entire message payload was a single PNG attachment named image002.png with no body text, no links, no macros, and no OCR text extracted.
  • The article states that the sending account was compromised and that the final-hop authentication pattern reflected the attacker sending through the victim’s infrastructure.
  • IRONSCALES’ Themis engine flagged the message at 54% confidence as credential theft, and one mailbox was quarantined.

SBOMs in 2026: Everyone's generating them, no one's using them — May Read

ENISA’s 2026 SBOM report shows that while regulation is pushing many organizations to generate SBOMs, most still struggle to use them for real vulnerability and supplier-risk management. The biggest gap is turning SBOMs from compliance paperwork into continuously monitored, actionable security data.

  • ENISA’s SBOM Adoption State of Play 2026 survey found that the Cyber Resilience Act is the top driver of SBOM adoption, with 43% of organizations saying it significantly accelerated their SBOM investment.
  • Although most organizations now produce SBOMs, 44% report a moderate gap and 23% report a significant gap between generating SBOMs and actually using them.
  • Supplier SBOM availability remains limited, with 39% of organizations never receiving SBOMs for commercial software they buy and another 39% receiving them only rarely.
  • Organizations cite vulnerability matching and data quality as major SBOM challenges, with 58% flagging CPE/PURL alignment and false positives and 60% flagging incomplete components and identifiers.
  • The article states that the main challenge for 2026 will be SBOM utilization through automated generation, continuous updates, vulnerability workflows tied to SBOM data, and visibility into supplier software.

One Click to Compromise: ThreatLabz 2026 Phishing and Initial Access Report — Optional

Zscaler ThreatLabz reports that phishing volume is declining but becoming more targeted and effective, with attackers using AI-generated sites, encrypted delivery, and real-time MFA-bypassing techniques to turn single clicks into session compromise. Security teams should focus on reducing exposed entry points, inspecting encrypted traffic, and stopping credential and session abuse earlier in the attack chain.

  • Zscaler ThreatLabz observed phishing activity decline by about 20% year over year in both 2024 and 2025, while attackers shifted toward targeted, personalized lures that resemble routine work.
  • ThreatLabz identified 413,524 AI-generated site instances and flagged 37,447 of them, or 9.06%, as malicious, with Manus AI, Blackbox AI, and Anything AI among the attributed builders.
  • ThreatLabz found that 95.2% of phishing activity was delivered over encrypted channels, making TLS/SSL inspection important for detecting credential theft, session abuse, and malicious redirects.
  • ThreatLabz observed phishing kits using adversary-in-the-middle and browser-in-the-middle techniques to capture credentials and MFA codes during active login flows.
  • ThreatLabz recorded 89.9 million hostile interactions with external decoys in six months and logged more than 121,000 distinct AWS-hosted IPs probing customer environments.

Code is being written everywhere, and the device is the only constant — Optional

As developers increasingly write and approve code through Slack, AI agents, MCP servers, and other non-IDE interfaces, the developer device remains the critical attack surface where credentials and supply-chain risks converge. The article argues security controls must monitor installs and activity at the device level because traditional MDM and EDR often miss malicious packages, extensions, and agent-driven workflows until after compromise.

  • PostHog co-founder James Hawkins said the company is merging roughly as many pull requests through Slack as through its code editor, reflecting a shift toward dispersed coding interfaces.
  • The article says developers are increasingly writing code through AI agents, chat interfaces, and MCP servers that many security teams have not seen or approved.
  • The article cites TeamPCP activity, the Axios maintainer account hijacking, and the Glassworm attack as examples of supply-chain incidents that targeted developer devices rather than vulnerabilities in application code.
  • Developer devices are described as holding cloud credentials, SSH keys, npm publish tokens, Kubernetes configs, source code access, and production access, making their compromise high-impact.
  • The article argues that MDM and EDR may not see or block malicious packages, IDE extensions, browser plugins, or postinstall hooks before credentials are stolen.

Zero Trust for AI Agents: How to Enforce Anthropic's Framework — Optional

Anthropic’s Zero Trust framework for AI agents is a strong starting point, but the article argues that real enforcement requires controls tied to the data layer. Varonis positions its Atlas platform as a way to discover, monitor, govern, and enforce AI agent security with data context.

  • Anthropic’s Zero Trust for AI Agents framework is organized around six pillars: agent identity and authentication, access control and privilege management, observability and auditing, behavioral monitoring and response, input validation and output controls, and integrity and recovery.
  • The article says AI agents change the security risk because they can bypass application controls, connect directly to databases, APIs, and data stores, and access data at machine speed.
  • Anthropic identifies prompt injection, tool poisoning, identity and privilege abuse, memory poisoning, and supply chain attacks as threats that distinguish agents from traditional IT systems.
  • The article argues that enforcing Zero Trust for AI agents requires data-layer context because an authenticated and authorized agent can still expose large amounts of over-permissioned sensitive data.
  • Varonis says its Atlas platform supports Zero Trust enforcement for AI agents through AI discovery, AI security posture management, runtime guardrails, compliance governance, activity monitoring, detection and response, and AI penetration testing.

Socket Partners with Replit to Block Malicious Packages in AI-Powered Development — Optional

Socket has partnered with Replit to integrate Socket Firewall into AI-powered development workflows, blocking malicious open source packages at install time. The goal is to protect builders from supply chain attacks such as typosquatting, credential stealers, and malicious transitive dependencies before code runs.

  • Socket Firewall is now built into Replit to evaluate open source packages as they are introduced into the build process.
  • The integration is intended to block threats such as typosquatted and impersonated packages, malicious transitive dependencies, credential stealers, and install scripts that fetch second-stage payloads.
  • The article says AI agents can now recommend, install, and wire open source packages into applications as part of the build process.
  • Socket says recent open source attacks can cause damage during installation before manual review can occur.
  • Replit CEO Amjad Masad said Replit and Socket are putting security in the building loop to block malicious code before it runs.

The Governance Gap: What IDC’s 2026 Data Reveals About AI and the Software Supply Chain — Optional

IDC’s 2026 data suggests enterprise AI adoption is moving faster than governance, leaving software supply chains exposed to unmanaged “Shadow AI” and risky AI-generated code. The key takeaway is that organizations need platform-level guardrails in delivery pipelines rather than relying on developers or AI agents to self-police compliance.

  • JFrog hosted a virtual panel discussion with IDC’s George Mironescu and JFrog executives Yuval Fernbach and Asaf Barkan about enterprise AI adoption, pipeline governance, and software delivery.
  • The IDC 2026 Software Engineering Challenges Report surveyed 1,000 enterprise and mid-sized organizations globally and identifies a widening gap between corporate AI ambition and governance readiness.
  • The article states that Shadow AI has replaced Shadow IT as the primary unmanaged threat inside enterprises, with developers using personal accounts, unvetted third-party plugins, and AI-generated code in production environments.
  • The article argues that AI agents cannot be trusted to self-police compliance because they operate non-deterministically and may bypass policies that exist only in documentation or verbal agreements.
  • The recommended path forward is to build platform-level guardrails at the software supply chain layer to manage AI adoption within corporate security and governance practices.

Your Zero Trust Policy Has an AI Agent Problem — Optional

AI agents undermine traditional zero trust assumptions because their valid credentials can be abused mid-session through prompt injection, MCP tool misuse, and uninspected app-to-LLM traffic. Organizations need visibility and policy enforcement for non-human identities and agent-to-tool/API communications, not just human login events.

  • Traditional zero trust controls evaluate identity, device attributes, and context at the moment of access, but AI agents can be compromised mid-session through malicious data they process after authenticating.
  • Prompt injection can cause an authorized AI agent with valid credentials to exfiltrate data, call unauthorized endpoints, or modify records without triggering policies built around authentication events.
  • Model Context Protocol traffic between AI agents and external tools or data sources may lack visibility, DLP enforcement, threat detection, and access control in existing security stacks.
  • App-to-LLM API traffic can flow directly between private infrastructure and AI models, bypassing cloud-based inspection points and leaving sensitive data exchanges uninspected.
  • Netskope One AI Security is presented as a way to inspect MCP traffic, enforce DLP policies on agentic workflows, assess MCP server risk, and monitor app-to-LLM API interactions.

Best Practices for Managing Third-Party Identity and Access Management — Optional

Health-ISAC released a white paper outlining best practices for managing third-party identity and access in healthcare, where vendors and partners often need remote access to network-connected systems and medical devices. The key takeaway is that healthcare organizations must tightly manage third-party accounts, roles, and privileges to reduce IAM-related risk.

  • Health-ISAC released “Best Practices for Managing Third-Party Identity and Access Management,” the 12th white paper in its ongoing IAM series for CISOs.
  • The white paper focuses on managing vendors, partners, and other third parties appropriately to minimize risk to healthcare organizations.
  • The article says healthcare organizations rely on third parties for many use cases because they have complex facilities, maintenance needs, security requirements, and network-connected devices.
  • The article notes that many healthcare systems and devices contain microprocessors and connect to networks, requiring remote access by third parties.
  • The paper will break down third-party IAM challenges, define best practices for health sector organizations, and use real-world scenarios to show how those practices apply.

The 5 Best Practices for Secure Identity Verification — Optional

Organizations should strengthen identity verification by moving beyond passwords and weak MFA toward phishing-resistant authentication, secure helpdesk verification, device trust, passkeys, and protected biometric workflows. The article frames these controls as essential to reducing credential theft and social-engineering-driven account takeovers.

  • The article says credential theft surged by 160% in 2025 and contributed to one in five data breaches as attackers used AI-driven attacks to bypass traditional defenses.
  • Organizations should use strong, fatigue-resistant multi-factor authentication and prioritize phishing-resistant methods such as FIDO2 security keys, passkeys, or certificate-based authentication over legacy SMS or email-based one-time passcodes.
  • Helpdesks are frequent targets for social engineering because attackers impersonate employees to request password resets, MFA changes, or other sensitive account actions.
  • Device trust can strengthen identity verification by evaluating signals such as whether a device is corporate-managed, its patch status, endpoint protection, device certificates, browser reputation, and signs of compromise.
  • The article recommends protecting biometric data by avoiding raw biometric storage where possible, using encrypted biometric templates, and performing authentication locally on trusted devices where feasible.

Ivanti, Fortinet, and SAP Release Patches for Multiple Critical Vulnerabilities — Optional

Fortinet, Ivanti, and SAP released patches for multiple critical flaws that could enable remote code execution, admin account creation, information disclosure, and unauthorized access. Organizations using affected FortiSandbox, Ivanti Sentry, SAP NetWeaver/ABAP, Commerce Cloud, or Data Hub products should update promptly, though no active exploitation has been reported.

  • Fortinet patched CVE-2026-25089, a CVSS 9.1 command injection vulnerability in FortiSandbox, FortiSandbox Cloud, and FortiSandbox PaaS WEB UI that could allow unauthenticated attackers to execute unauthorized commands via crafted HTTP requests.
  • Ivanti released fixes for CVE-2026-10520 and CVE-2026-10523 in Ivanti Sentry, which could allow remote unauthenticated attackers to achieve root-level remote code execution or create arbitrary administrative accounts.
  • watchTowr Labs said CVE-2026-10520 could be exploited through a specially crafted HTTP request to the /mics/api/v2/sentry/mics-config/handleMessage endpoint, and Ivanti’s patch blocks unauthenticated access to that endpoint.
  • SAP issued fixes for four critical vulnerabilities affecting NetWeaver AS ABAP and ABAP Platform, SAP Commerce Cloud, SAP Data Hub, and SAP NetWeaver Application Server Java.
  • The article states there is no evidence that any of the listed vulnerabilities have been exploited in the wild.

Your Automated Pentest Looks Clean. See What It Missed in This Expert Webinar — Optional

A clean automated pentest report can create false confidence because it may only show that attack paths were tested, not whether security controls detected or blocked them. The webinar argues teams should pair automated pentesting with broader control validation, such as BAS, to prioritize risks based on what actually gets caught.

  • The article promotes a The Hacker News webinar with Picus Security about gaps in automated pentesting validation.
  • The article says a stable or clean automated pentest report can mean the tool has reached the edge of what it can see rather than that the environment is secure.
  • Picus frames validation as six surfaces and places automated pentesting on the attack-path surface, leaving areas such as detection rules, cloud configurations, identity controls, and AI guardrails unproven.
  • The article states that automated pentesting can show whether credential dumping or lateral movement is possible but cannot show whether a SIEM rule fired, an EDR raised an alert, or a SOC had enough signal to act.
  • The article says breach and attack simulation asks whether a control blocks, detects, logs, or misses known behavior, while automated pentesting asks how far an attacker could get through an exploitable path.
entry.summary: This 24-hour digest is dominated by active exploitation, record vulnerability volume, and continued abuse of developer ecosystems. Reporting also highlights how AI-enabled development, agentic workflows, and software supply-chain complexity are expanding the attack surface across identity, data,...

entry.title: Ivanti Sentry CVE-2026-10520 RCE and Microsoft Defender RoguePlanet Zero-Day
entry.content: This digest centers on two high-impact vulnerability disclosures affecting widely deployed enterprise security technologies: Ivanti Sentry and Microsoft Defender. The reporting highlights a pre-authentication root-level remote code execution path in Ivanti Sentry and a publicly released Microsoft Defender local privilege escalation exploit capable of spawning SYSTEM shells on fully patched Windows systems. WatchTowr Labs published technical analysis of CVE-2026-10520, an OS command injection vulnerability in Ivanti Sentry versions before R10.5.2, R10.6.2, and R10.7.1. According to the analysis, the vulnerable unauthenticated endpoint is /mics/api/v2/sentry/mics-config/handleMessage, where a user-controlled message parameter is passed into backend configuration handling logic. The issue can allow a remote unauthenticated attacker to achieve root-level remote code execution. The researchers described how the message value is parsed as a MICS configuration command into fields including command, module, XPath, and XML body. They found that the EXECUTE path could reach executeNativeCommand through reflection-based backend handling, and reproduced the vulnerability by sending a POST request containing a commandexec XML block with uname -a in the reqandres field. The HTTP response returned command output, demonstrating practical exploitation of the flaw. The patched Ivanti implementation reportedly hardcodes the command passed to handleMessage and adds Apache configuration rules that block access to the vulnerable endpoint, redirecting unauthenticated requests to the login page. The analysis is notable because it provides endpoint-level and execution-path detail for a vulnerability that sits on an exposed management/security product boundary, where unauthenticated reachability materially increases operational risk. BleepingComputer reported on RoguePlanet, a Microsoft Defender zero-day exploit released by researcher Nightmare Eclipse. The exploit is described as a race condition that can spawn a command prompt with SYSTEM privileges on fully patched Windows 10 and Windows 11 devices, though its reliability varies by system. ThreatLocker told BleepingComputer it reproduced the exploit on fully patched Windows 11 systems with KB5094126 installed and confirmed it worked as described. Nightmare Eclipse said RoguePlanet was originally developed as a remote code execution vulnerability involving Microsoft Defender’s handling of files on remote SMB shares. The researcher claimed Microsoft silently hardened Defender in mid-May to block junction attacks, changing the exploit’s practical impact from the originally described RCE scenario to a local privilege escalation path. The release also forms part of an ongoing public dispute between Nightmare Eclipse and Microsoft over vulnerability disclosure and bug bounty practices, following previous releases named BlueHammer, RedSun, GreenPlasma, and YellowKey. Together, these disclosures underscore continued scrutiny of defensive and mobile infrastructure products as high-value exploit targets. One case involves unauthenticated command execution in an enterprise gateway product, while the other concerns privilege escalation in a default Windows security component, creating distinct but operationally significant exposure patterns for defenders tracking exploit development and public proof-of-concept activity.
entry.summary: This digest centers on two high-impact vulnerability disclosures affecting widely deployed enterprise security technologies: Ivanti Sentry and Microsoft Defender. The reporting highlights a pre-authentication root-level remote code execution path in Ivanti Sentry and a publicly released Microsoft...
